text: CHOUISTAMNO, a town of Russia, in the gov. of Viburg, 21 m. NNE of Serdobol, on the N bank of a small lake. Pop. 2,500.
CHOUL, a town of Hindostan, on the coast, 30 m. S of Bombay, in the raja of Colaba's territories.
CHOULESBURY, a parish in Bucks, 4 m. NNW of Chesham. Area 170 acres. Pop. 124.
CHOUMLA. See SHUMLA.
CHOURAGURH, a town and fortress of Hindostan, in Gundwana, at the N extremity of the Mahadeo hills, in N lat. 22° 48'.

CHOURMA, a village of Russia in Europe, in the gov. and 114 m. of Viatka, district and 17 m. SE of Ourjoum, on an affluent of the Viatka. In the vicinity are the forges of Chourmansk and Chourmanikolsk.
CHOUR-MOUNTSANG-LA, a mountain-chain in Tibet, in Tsang or Ulterior Tibet, to the SE of Teshu-Lumbu, and on the N of Ding-ghie, Founghia, and Nielan.
CHOUTRA, a village of Northern India, in the Punjab, on the r. bank of the Jelum, near its confluence with the Chenab.

CHOUZE'-SUR-LOIRE, a commune and town of France, in the dep. of the Indre-et-Loire, cant. of Bourgueil, 8 m. NW of Chinon, on the r. bank of the Loire. Pop. in 1841, 3,852. It has a considerable trade in corn, dried fruits, staves, &c.
CHOUZY, a commune of France, in the dep. of the Loire-et-Cher, cant. of Herbault, 8 m. from Blois, on the l. bank of the Cisse. Pop. 1,019.
CHOWAL, a district of Hindostan, in the prov. of Gujerat. It is watered by the Rupeyne and other minor streams, and contains the towns of Bejapore, Massawna, and Maunsa. CHR

CHOWAN, a county in the state of Carolina, U.S., comprising an area of 200 sq. m., bounded on the W by a river of the same name, and on the S by Albemarle sound. It presents a level surface, but is generally productive. Pop. in 1830, 6,688; in 1840, 6,693, of whom 2,865 were whites, 3,665 slaves, and 160 free coloured. Cap., Edenton.—The river C., formed by the junction of the Nottaway, Meherrin, and Blackwater rivers, which rise and run nearly their entire course in the state of Virginia, flows ESE until it reaches the county of the same name, when it bends S, and enters Albemarle sound, to the N of the mouth of the Roanoke, from which it is separated by Chowan point. The Meherrin branch is navigable for large vessels to Murfreesboro, in the co. of Hert-
CHOWKA, a village and pass of Hindostan, in the prov. and 10 m. NE of the city of Aurungabad.
CHOWLEY, a township in the p. of Coddington, Cheshire. Area 580 acres. Pop. 77.
CHOWRY, an island of the Indian ocean, in the Nicobar group, to the NNW of Therese island, in N lat. 8° 27'. It is about 3 m. in circumf., and presents a level surface.
CHRAST, a town of Bohemia, in the circle and 8 m. SE of Chrudim. Pop. 1,648.
CHRAUSTOWITZ, a village of Bohemia, in the circle of Chrudim, between Chrudim and Hohenmauth. It contains about 100 houses.

CHRICO-MOLA, a river of Costa Rica in Central America, which falls into the SW part of Chiriqui lagune, opposite Carl island, after a S course of about 50 m. It is navigable for about 25 m., and flows through a fertile though uncultivated district.
CHRISHALL, a parish in Essex, 5 m. N of Clavering. Area 2,690 acres. Pop. 521.

CHRISTBURG, a town of Prussia, in the gov. of Marienwerder, circle of Stuhm, on the Sorge river, 12 m. SSE of Marienburg. Pop. in 1837, 2,369. It has manufactories of woollen and cotton cloth, and some tanneries, distilleries, and brass-foundries; and conducts a considerable trade in grain.

CHRISTCHURCH, a parish in Monmouthshire, 2½ ENE of Newport. Area 5,320 acres. Pop. 1,310. -Also a p. and parl. borough in Hants, 21 m. SW of Southampton, on the borders of the New Forest, and a little above the junction of the Avon and the Stour. Area of the p. 24,640 acres. Pop. 5,994. The church is a large and ancient building, exhibiting the dimensions and ground-plan of a cathedral. Pop. of the parl. borough, which includes the chapelry of Holdenhurst, and returns one member, 6,634. Registered electors in 1847, 315.

CHRISTIAN, a county in the S part of Kentucky, U. S., intersected by Little river, Pond, and Tradewater rivers. Area 612 sq. m. Pop. 15,587. The cap. is Hopkinsville.—Also a county in the central part of the state of Illinois, watered by the S fork of Sangamon river. Area 350 sq. m. Pop. 1,878. The cap. is Edinburg. - Also a township in Independence co. in Arkansas. Pop. 425.

CHRISTIAN, or WEST UPLAND'S AMT, an administrative district of Norway, in the stift of Aggershuus or Christiania, between the parallels of 60° 10' and 62° 20' N. It is a mountainous district, rising in the N into the chain of the Dovre-field and the Sognefield, and comprising a considerable portion of the Gulbrandsdalen valley. The Sneehättan [alt. 7,489 ft.] is the culminating point in this district, which has a superficial area of 9,461 English sq. m., or, according to Norwegian admeasurement, 6,415,000 tons, of which only 195,030 are arable; and this surface, in respect of alt. above sea-level, is divided into 51,950 tons, having an elevation of from 300 to 600 ft.; 65,960 tons ranging from 600 to 850 ft.; and 77,120 tons above 850 ft. The produce of the soil in 1830 was 189 tondern of wheat; 8,763 of rye; 92,229 barley; 51,366 mixed corn; 8,456 oats; 3,954 pease; 133,289 potatoes. The amount of stock held in that year within this amt was 12,533 horses; 68,062 cattle; 86,684 sheep; 22,009 goats; and 7,521 pigs. There are 4 glass-works in the amt, which produce, on an average of 5 years, to the value of 614,571 rix dollars. Pop. in 1801, 66,281; in 1825, 83,103; in 1835, 95,182. Of the last-mentioned pop. 6,128 were returned as udalmen or proprietors, and 625 as farmers but not proprietors; 29 as clergy; 16 merchants; 96 manufacturers; 1,797 artisans; 4,398 day-labourers; 7,583 domestic servants; and 3,276 paupers. - This amt is divided into 3 fogderi or bailiwicks, and 68 parishes. Its only town is Lillehammer. The S part appears, in ancient times, to have borne the name of Greenland, from its fertility.

CHRISTIANA, a river of the United States, which has its head-branches in the states of Pennsylvania and Maryland, and flows into the Brandywine at Wilmington. It is navigable for vessels drawing 9 ft. water to Newport, and for those drawing only 6 ft. to Christiana, 9 m. above Wilmington.

CHRISTIANA, or CHRISTIANA-BRIDGE, a township and v. in Newcastle co., in the state of Delaware, U. S., on the Christiana river, 9 m. SW of Wilmington. Pop. 3,725. CHRISTIANA, CHRISTIANES, OF KOUPHONISA Isles, a group of 4 islets in the Mediterranean, 12 m. SW of Santorin, in N lat. 36° 15'.

CHRISTIANIA, the capital of Norway, situated on the bay of Björwigen, at the bottom of the gulf of C., at the embouchure of the Agger, in N lat. 59° 54' 42', E long. 10° 49', 162 m. ESE of Bergen. The Agger, or Aggers-elv, is a large stream, which, coming from the N, flows down a declivity of 5 m. in a succession of falls and rapids, till it passes through the city, and empties itself into the long fiord or gulf of C., at the extremity of which the city stands. This fiord has a general direction of 60 m. N; but near its head makes a short bend towards the E, and the range of hills which form its E bank dip their precipitous sides in the water directly facing the city, while the range on the W side takes a sweep round to the NW and N, where it appears rugged and lofty, and the broad valley below it is lost in the distance to the NE. The houses of the town are built of brick or stone: few of them are above two stories high, and though neat, they present no striking appearance. The streets are broad, regular, and wellpaved; but incline from each side to the centre of the street, which thus becomes the common drain. The suburbs of the city are almost on a level with the swampy grounds, covered with samphire and seaweed, around which they are built. The approach to all the houses is by a flight of steps rising above the lower story. The cathedral is a plain building; and the original college, except from its size, could not be distinguished from a private house, but a suite of superb buildings are now in the course of erection for this establishment. Few of the public buildings, not even the palace, possess any great external decoration. One of the most conspicuous buildings is the jail, which is finely situated on a rising ground. The largest open place is the market, which on a market day is crowded with the country-people and their wares, chiefly wood and provisions. The fortress is a compact building, overlooking the sea from a commanding site. Running nearly from the quay to the castle is a raised terrace parallel with the sea, which forms the principal promenade. The observatory, lying at a little distance from the town, on the road to Drammen, is a quiet unobtrusive building. Among seum, an hospital, a military academy, a grammar school, and an academy of commerce. The university, founded in 1811 by Frederick VI. of Denmark, had 20 professors and 7 lecturers, in 1840, and was attended by 700 students. The public library contained 126,000 vols. and 8,000 MSS. in 1840. There were 12 printing-offices in C. in 1849; and about 100 vols., including however only a few original works, are annually published here. Dr. E. D. Clarke reported that in 1801 there was not in all Norway one bookseller's shop; with the exception of a few bookbinders and stationers' shops in C. and Tronÿem where one might purchase bibles, prayer-books, or almanacs; but Mr. R. Chambers found the book and music-shops in C., in 1849, both numerous, and "of the first class in point of appearance." As the capital of the kingdom, the storthing meets every three years in C., in the month of February. It is also the residence of the stiftsamtmann and the bishop; and of the council-of-state who compose the regency of the kingdom, consisting of the viceroy or governor-general, and the heads of the departments of finance, religion, justice, and the army and navy. -The pop. of the city in 1801 was 9,699; in 1833, 20,850; in 1849, 33,000. The chief manufactures of C. are woollens, tobacco, glass, soap, leather, and cordage. Some cotton factories and iron-works have been recently established here. The harbour has 6 to 7 fath. water close to the quay; and the long fiord by which it is connected with the Skager-rack, though in many parts narrow and of intricate navigation, has deep water throughout. Its commerce consists in the exportation of timber, iron, iron utensils, copper, alum, glass, tar, and skins. The deals or timber-planks of this port have always been held in greater estimation than those from any other part of Norway: this has been chiefly owing to the careful manner in which they are cut at the sawing-mills. In 1792, when the pop. of C. had not reached 10,000, 521 ships left its port laden with deals. In 1817, the export to England of timber was only 79 cargoes; and in 1843, the exportation of timber to Great Britain was chiefly confined to firewood, and rough timber for the use of miners, on which the duties in England were moderate. The number of vessels which entered the port of C. in <math>1842</math> was <math>570 = 59,661</math> tons, of which only 1 = 93 tons was British. The ports of Tonsberg, Laurvig, and Christiansand, all within the consulate of C., are more frequented by British shipping. The chief timber trade is now conducted with France. A considerable quantity of salted and smoked herrings is exported to the Prussian ports. -A railroad has been projected between C. and Lake Miosen, the length of which will be 37 m., and which will bring C. into communication with Lillehammer at the N extremity of that lake, and the great forest-districts beyond. The transport of timber and of forest-produce, along this line, may be expected to be considerable. C., although the smallest of the capitals of Europe, says Mr. Inglis, "is certainly one of the most interesting to a stranger; and, in situation, far exceeds them all in the romantic beauties by which it is surrounded. The fiord upon which it stands is so dotted with wooded islands, and forms so many curves and indentures, that it has more the appearance of a fresh-water lake than an arm of the sea: especially as the heights, which enclose four-fifths of its circumf., preserve its surface unruffled. When large vessels in full sail are seen threading their way among these islets, it may easily be supposed that the effect is singularly novel and beautiful. I have never seen, nor do I believe there exists, a happier the public institutions are a botanic garden, a mu- | combination of images than that which is presented on a summer's day from the heights above C. If a stranger could be conveyed by magic, and placed on the height of Egeberg on an evening in July, and were asked in what part of the world he supposed himself to be, he would more probably name Italy or Greece than the icy region of Scandinavia. The bay itself, with its romantic promontories and wooded isles, may vie with Como; and in the country which stretches on every side of the town, we are struck with the extraordinary combination of rich, riant, and picturesque beauty. Corn fields, copses, gardens, lawns, cottages, and villas, lie beautifully blended beneath as warm a sky as canopies more southern lands. Below lie the blue waters of the fiord, reflecting the fantastic and wood - crowned heights that environ it; while, every now and then, tall masts and white sails appear and disappear among its leafy isles; and beyond, to the north and west, heights rise into hills, and hills into mountains; while overtopping them all, ridges of snow, purpled in the light of evening, form the majestic boundary of this wondrous amphitheatre."-"The climate of C. is better than that of any other part of Norway. C. is neither subject to the winds which assail the S coast, nor the rains which are prevalent along the whole of the W coast, from Stavanger to Drontheim. The fiord, at the head of which C. stands, has a considerable effect in moderating the extreme severity of winter, without adding more to the moisture of the climate than is sufficient to increase the fertility of the land. By comparative observations made at Bergen, Drontheim, and C., it appears that the cold is more intense during winter, and that winter is of somewhat longer continuance at C. than at Bergen, which lies nearly in the same lat., but upon the N Atlantic ocean; and that at C. the therm. generally falls as low during some part of the winter—usually in January—as it does at Drontheim, which lies 210 farther N. But the winter at Drontheim is of longer continuance than at C. During at least two months in the year, the main branch of the fiord is frozen about one-third of the distance from C. to the sea; and all the smaller branches which stretch from the sides are frozen for a considerably longer time. The harbour of C. is locked up generally between three and four months; and the ships are still icebound, when the country around has begun to exhibit strong appearances of spring. Stoves begin to be lighted in C. about the middle of September, and cannot be dispensed with until the middle of May." The mean temp. of the year at St. Petersburg is +3° R., or 39° 25 F.; while that of C., under the same parallel, is +5° R., or 43.25 F.
CHRISTIANIA (PROVINCE OF). See AGGERHUUS.
CHRISTIAN-MALFORD, a parish in Wilts, 5 m. from Chippenham. Area 2,810 acres. Pop. 1,179.
CHRISTIANOPEL, a town and port of Sweden, on the Baltic, in the laen and 16 m. NE of Carlscrona.

CHRISTIANSAND, a stift or province of Norway, forming the SW extremity of the kingdom, lying between the parallels of 58° and 60° N, and bounded on the N by Bergen and Aggerhuus; on the E by Aggerhuus; on the S by the Skager-rack; and on the W by the North sea. It comprises the amts of Nedenäs, Mandal, Stavanger, and Bradsberg, and the fogden of Ovra-Tellemarken. Its length from SW to NE is 135 m.; its average breadth is 100 m. Its superficial area is 13,618 English sq. m., or 9,234,000 tons, of which only 140,000 tons are arable. The Norska-fjellen range intersects this district from SW to NE, terminating on the coast in Cape Lindesnaes, and spreading out towards the NE into various ranges, amongst which are the CHR Jokle-field, the Hougle-field [alt. 4,668 ft.], the Gute-field [alt. 4,778 ft.], the Broke-field [alt. 4,188 ft.], and the Gousta-fell [alt. 6,477 ft.]. Among these lofty ridges lie several long and narrow lakes, most of which discharge themselves by rapid streams flowing S towards the Skager-rack. The principal rivers are the Torrisdals-elf and the Nid-elf. The W coast is deeply indented by the Bukke and the Stavanger fiords. Cultivation is chiefly confined to the coast districts; the principal wealth of the country is drawn from its immense forests of oak, pine, and birch.—The pop. in 1769 was returned at 113,024; in 1801, at 133,515; in 1835, at 194,599.—The principal towns are Grimstad, Lillesand, Christiansand, Mandal, Egersund, and Stavanger.

CHRISTIANSAND, the cap. of the above prov., and of the amt of Mandal, is situated upon a small bay of the same name, on the N coast of the Skager-rack, in N lat. 58° 8′ 5″, E long. 8° 3′, at the mouth of the Torridals-elf, 220 m. NW of Copenhagen, 174 m. SW of Christiania. It is neatly laid out in long broad streets, in which the houses are separated from one another by gardens. The cathedral is the principal building. The port is good, and is defended by several batteries, and a fort on Christiansholm a small islet. Tobacco, brandy, and sail-cloth, are the chief articles of manufacture. The exports consist of wood, oak-bark, herrings, goat-skins, stone moss, dried fish, smoked salmon, and lobsters. Pop. in 1801, 4,787; in 1841, 8,139.
CHRISTIANSBORG. See Accra.
CHRISTIANSBURG, a town of Oldenburg, in the circle of Neuenburg, 20 m N of Oldenburg, near the coast.

CHRISTIANSBURG, the cap. of Montgomery co., in the state of Virginia, U. S., 203 m. WSW of Richmond. Pop. 335. - Also a v. in Shelby co., Kentucky, 13 m. W by N of Frankfort.—Also a v. in Ohio, in the township of Jackson, 60 m. W by N of Columbus.

CHRISTIANSFELD, a town of Denmark, in Sleswig, 6 m. N of Hadersleben, in N lat. 55° 21' 36". It was founded in 1773 by a colony of Hernhutters or Moravian brethren, and is a place of considerable industry. Its manufactures consist of leather, gloves, woollen stuffs, and hosiery. Pop. 800.
CHRISTIANSOE. See ERDHOLMS.

CHRISTIANSSTED, or BAIRN-END, a town in the Danish island of Santa Cruz, on the NE coast. Pop. 5,500. It is well-built, and fortified; and is the residence of the governor-general of the Danish Antilles. The harbour is difficult of access from numerous reefs.—About 1 m. to the ESE of the town, on the summit of a hill 440 ft. in alt., is a private observatory, the lat. of which, 17° 44′ 32″, is considered to be true to within one second; and the long. 64° 40′ 43", the result of the labour of years, may be held to approach within 4" in time, or 1' in arc, of absolute accuracy.

CHRISTIANSTAD, a laen or province in the S of Sweden, lying between the parallels of 55° 23' and 56° 32' N; and bounded on the N by the laens of Halmstad, Wexio, and Carlskrona; on the E by the laen of Carlskrona, and the Baltic sea; on the S by the Baltic, and the laen of Malmoehus; and on the W by Malmochus and the Cattegat. It has an area of 55.35 Swedish, or 2,430 English sq. m., and nearly one-half of its surface has an elevation exceeding 300 ft. above sea-level. In 1828 about one-tenth of the surface was under cultivation; one-eighth, in meadows and pasturages; and one-sixth, in lakes and marshes; the remainder consisted of mountains and uncultivated land. It was, when formerly part of the kingdom of Denmark, in a better state of cultivation. A small portion of the soil is a deep ~ rich loam, which yields good crops of wheat; but; rye and barley are more extensively grown.—Pop. in 1751, 90,335; in 1839, 162,809. The density of pop. in 1839 was nearly 66.5 per sq. m.; that for all Sweden being 18.7.—It is subdivided into 4 fogderi or bailiwicks, and 149 parishes. The principal towns are C., Cimbrishamn, and Engelholm.

CHRISTIANSTAD, the cap. of the above province, is situated in N lat. 56° 1', in a plain on the Helge river, at the point where it spreads into a small lake, and 9 m. from its embouchure. It is a small place, but is neatly built, and was formerly well-fortified. It conducts some commerce, by means of its port Ahuus at the mouth of the Helge, in wood, alum, and potash. The river itself is navigable only for small craft of 7 tons.—The town was founded in 1614 by Christian IV. of Denmark, when this prov. belonged to the Danes. It was the scene of the debut in arms of the great Gustavus Adolphus who won it from the Danes; and was finally ceded to the Swedes by the peace of Roskild in 1658. It has a small garrison as a military arsenal.
CHRISTIANSTADT, a town of Prussia, in Brandenburg, in the circle and 11 m. NNE of Sorau, on the Bober. Pop. in 1837, 826.

CHRISTIANSUND, a town and port of Norway, in the amt of Romsdal, built upon three small islands named Kirkeland, Nordland, and Inland, in the German ocean, in N lat. 63° 7', 87 m. WSW of Drontheim. Pop. in 1801, 1,624; in 1833, 2,320. It conducts an active commerce in fish, barilla, lichens, and wood. In 1842, 86 vessels = 9,308 tons arrived in this port, of which 10 = 1,442 tons were British.
CHRISTIANSVILLE, a village of Mecklenburg co., in the state of Virginia, U. S., 97 m. SW of Richmond.—Also a v. in Carroll co., in Tennessee, 113 m. W of Nashville.
CHRISTINA-DE-VALMADRIGAL, a town of Spain, in the prov. and 21 m. SSE of Leon.

CHRISTINA (SANTA), or TAHUATA, an island in the S division of the Marquesas group, 23 m. E of the island of Dominica. Captain Cook estimates its length from N to S at 9 m.; and its circumf. at 27 m. It is thinly clad with vegetation, and for the most part covered with a low barren shrub. The N part of the island appears to be the most fertile. A narrow chain of mountains extends throughout its whole length; in which the highest point attains an alt. estimated at 2,009 ft. All the districts which Forster and Sparrman visited were found covered with a rich mould, resting on rocks which contained volcanic productions. Marchand enumerates the plantain, the cocoa-nut, a species of apple, the yam, ginger, cucumbers, gourds, a species of edible chestnut equal in size to six Castile chestnuts, and a species of walnut, as among the vegetable producductions of this island. The natives are of a deep bright brown colour, but as there is scarcely a spot on their bodies that is not tattooed, their skin appears nearly black. They are a fine muscular race of people, with good features, high foreheads, fine eyes and teeth, a high nose, and long soft hair of various hues, as flaxen, auburn, or black. All wear earrings of bone, boars' tusks, or shells; and, on their wrists and ankles, bracelets of human hair. A single cloth fastened round the waist, and between the thighs, is their only clothing. They subsist almost exclusively on bread-fruit; but eat uncooked fish with avidity. Pigs and poultry may be procured in abundance here. Their language is a harsh guttural jargon, spoken in a kind of stuttering bellow. Marchand—who gives specimens of it—says it has a great affinity to that of the Sandwich islands. Resolution bay—the bay of La Madre de Dios of Quiros CHR -on the W coast, lying S 15° E from the W point of Dominica, affords good anchorage; but there is an almost continual surf on the beach. The officers of the Actaon, who visited this island in 1836, report that it is high water in this bay "every day precisely at noon."
CHRISTINEHAMN, a town of Sweden, in the laen and 18 m. ESE of Carlstadt, on the Varn, a little above its entrance into the Wener lake. Pop. in 1833, 1,759. It has an active trade in iron.

CHRISTINESTAD, or CHRISTINA, a town and port of Russia, in Finland, in the laen and 60 m. S of Wasa, on the gulf of Bothnia, in N lat. 62° 16'. Pop. 1,700. The port is good; and a considerable amount of commercial traffic in pitch, butter, tallow, timber, wood-work, and cattle, is conducted from this place.
CHRISTIONYDD-KENRICK, a township in the p. of Ruabon, in Denbighshire, 2 m. N of Chick. Pop. in 1821, 3,107; in 1841, 4,554. There are extensive iron and coal works here.
CHRISTLETON, a parish and township in Cheshire, 3 m. SE by E of Chester. Area of p. 3,070 acres. Pop. 875. Area of t. 1,480 acres. Pop. 728.
CHRISTMAS ISLAND, or ISLE-DE-NOEL, an island in the Pacific, in N lat. 1° 45', W long. 162°, discovered by Cook in 1777.
CHRISTMAS SOUND, a channel on the S coast of Tierra-del-Fuego, between Rous peninsula on the E and Waterman island on the W, intersected by the parallel of 55° 35' S, and the meridian of 69° 58' W.
CHRISTO-EN-JARRET (SAINT), a commune and v. of France, in the dep. of Loire, cant. of Saint- Heant. Pop. 1,716.

CHRISTOBAL (SAN), or SAN CHRISTOVAL, a town of Mexico, in the prov. and 25 m. NNW of Mexico, on the l. bank of a lake of the same name. The lake, which is 12 m. in length, and 6 m. in breadth, is connected with the lake of Mexico on the S by a stream about 1 m. in length, and by the Desagne or Tula with the gulf of Mexico.-Also a village in the Mexican prov. of Guadalaxara, near the junction of the Rio-Guichipila with the Rio-Grande. Pop. 300.—Also a town of New Grenada, in the prov. of Antioquia, in N lat. 6° 16', at an alt. of 5,920 ft. above sea-level. Pop. 1,165.—Also a town of Venezuela, in the prov. and 75 m. SW of Merida, on an affluent of the Orivante. Pop. 3,000. It has a good weekly market, and an active trade in cacao, honey, sugar, and other articles of local produce. The climate is temperate. C. was founded by Maldonado in 1560.—Also a town of Bolivia, in the intendancy and 100 m. SW of Potosi, on an affluent of the Pilcomayo.—Also a town 155 m. SW of Potosi, on the San Juan.—Also a town 30 m. NE of Potosi, on an affluent of the Paspaya.

CHRISTOBAL-DE-BUEDO (SAN), a town of Spain, in Leon, in the prov. and 38 m. N of Palencia, partido of Saldana, near the l bank of the Buedo. CHRISTOBAL-DE-LA-HABANA (SAN). See HAVANNAH. CHRISTOBAL-DE-LA-LAGUNA (SAN). See LA LAGUNA.
CHRISTOL (SAINT), a commune and v. of France, in the dep. of Herault, cant. of Lunel. Pop. 656.
CHRISTOLY (SAINT), a commune and village of France, in the dep. of Gironde, cant. of Saint-Savin. Pop. 1,903.
CHRISTON, a parish in Somersetshire, 4 m. NW of Axbridge. Area 350 acres. Pop. 91.

CHRISTOPHE (SAINT), a commune of France, in the dep. of Cantal, cant. of Pleaux, 10 m. S of Mauriac. Pop. 1,067. It contains coal-mines.— Also a commune in the dep. of the Charente-Inferieure, cant. of La Jarrie. Pop. 1,028. - Also a commune and town in the dep. of the Indre-et- Loire, cant. of Neuvy-Le-Roy, 21 m. NNW of Tours. Pop. 1,522.

CHRISTOPHE (SAINT), a canton and commune of France, in the dep. of the Indre, arrond. of Issoudun. The cant. comprises 11 com. Pop. in 1831, 9,681; in 1841, 10,090. The village, called also Saint-Christophe-en-Bazele, is 23 m. NW of Issoudun. Pop. 523.
CHRISTOPHE-EN-BRESSE (SAINT), a hamlet of France, in the dep. of the Saone-et-Loire, cant. of Saint-Germain-du-Plain, and com. of L'Abergement- Saint-Colombe. Pop. 1,166.
CHRISTOPHE - EN - BRIONNAIS (SAINT), a commune of France, in the dep. of the Saône-et- Loire, cant. of Semur-en-Brionnais, 13 m. SSW of Charolles. Pop. 1,281.
CHRISTOPHE-DE-CONFOLENS (SAINT), a commune of France, in the dep. of the Charente, cant. and 10 m. E of Confolens. Pop. 1,212.
CHRISTOPHE-SUR-DOLAISON (SAINT), or

SAINT - CHRISTOPHE - DU - VELAY, a commune of France, in the dep. of the Haut-Loire, cant. of Solignac-Sur-Loire. Pop. 1,046. CHRISTOPHE - ENTRE - DEUX - GUIERS, (SAINT), a commune of France, in the dep. of the Isère, cant. of Saint-Laurent-du-Pont. Pop. 1,300.
CHRISTOPHE - DU - LIGNERON (SAINT), a commune of France, in the dep. of Vendée, cant. of Palluau, 27 m. N of Les Sables. Pop. 1,685.
CHRISTOPHE-DU-LUAT (SAINT), a commune of France, in the dep. of Mayenne, cant. of Evron. Pop. 1,150.
CHRISTOPHE-DU-PIN (SAINT). See LE PIN.

CHRISTOPHER'S (SAINT), or SAINT KITT'S, an island in the West Indies, in N lat. 17° 16', W long. 62° 31', 50 m. W by N from Antigua, 30 m. S of Saint Bartholomew, and separated from Nevis on the SE by a strait only 1 m. wide. It was discovered in 1493 by Columbus, who bestowed upon it his own Christian name. It is of an irregular oblong figure, about 20 m. in length from NW to SE, and in general about 5 m. in breadth. Towards the SE extremity it is not more than 3 m. in breadth; and between that part and the rest of the island is a strip of land 3 m. in length, which does not measure half-a-mile across. It contains 43,726 acres of land, of which about 17,000 acres are appropriated to the growth of sugar, and 4,000 to pasturage. Sugar, with the exception of provisions, and a little cotton and indigo, was, until recently, the only species of produce raised; and it was estimated by Edwards that one-half of the whole island is unfit for cultivation. The China and the Seville orange grow luxuriantly, also the Shaddock or Forbidden fruit. The interior part of the country consists of rugged, precipitous, and barren mountains. Of these the loftiest is Mount Misery, which is evidently a decayed volcano, and raises its craggy perpendicular summit to the height of 3,711 ft. above the level of the sea. The inhabitants apply the name Mount Misery only to the large aggregate of rock on the summit, which rises at an average angle of about 50°. Brimstone hill, on the W side of the island, has an alt. of 715 ft. It is a mass of madrepore limestone. A nearly uniform range of country, presenting an enclosed plane sloping towards the sea, and intersected at intervals by deep ravines, interposes between the interior mountain-range and the sea-coast. In some of the intermediate spaces, between the heads of the mountains, are plains of considerable extent, nearly level, well covered with grass, and free from shrubs or wood, which afford good pasture. All the mountains are scored by deep ravines. Immense strata of volcanic ashes are everywhere found; which in some CHR places alternate with submarine productions. Nature has here made abundant amends for the comparative sterility of the mountains, by the fertility of the plains: no part of the West Indies possesses a soil superior to that of this island. It is in general a dark grey loam, so light and porous as to be penetrable by the slightest application of the hoe; and seems to consist of a black ferruginous pumice, finely incorporated with a pure loam or virgin mould, through which water percolates so quickly that the surface is quite dry within an hour after the heaviest rain. The under stratum is gravel from 8 to 12 inches deep. Clay is nowhere to be found except at a considerable height on the mountains. In the SE quarter are several salt-ponds; and many of the springs are impregnated with saline matter. The climate is dry and healthy. The mean temp. on the coast is 80°, but the considerable elevation of much of the land above sea-level and the absence of all stagnant waters, secures a genial atmosphere, especially during October, November, and December. St. C. constitutes-along with Nevis, Antigua, Montserrat, and the Virgin islands—a separate government, whose chief, styled Captain-general of the Leeward Caribbean islands, usually resides at Antigua, visiting the other islands occasionally. St. C. is governed by a lieutenant-governor, with a council of 10 members, and a house of assembly of 24 members. The revenue in 1836 was £4,173; the expenditure, £4,372. The island is divided into 9 parishes; and contains 4 towns, viz., Basseterre, Sandy Port, Old Row, and Deep Bay.—The two peaks of Brimstone hill have been strongly fortified.—The principal anchorage of the island is in Bassaterre roads. The SE side is exposed to S and SW winds, which are moreover the quarters in which hurricanes almost always commence. The principal articles of export are sugar, rum, molasses, coffee, arrow-root, and tobacco. Tue amount of sugar exported in 1831 was 101,968 cwt4.; in 1836, 64,810 cwts.; in 1841, 63,936 cwts.; in 1846, 10,396,624 lbs. The quantity of rum exported in 1837 was 87,380 gallons; in 1846, 62,801 gallons. The exportation of coffee, which in 1836 was only 605 lbs., in 1837 was 80,839 lbs. In 1836, the export of tobacco amounted to 12,540 lbs. The aggregate annual value of the produce of this island is about £750,000. The whole value of the exports in 1809 was £132,845; of imports, £266,066. In 1836, the exports amounted to £145,703; the imports to £98,344. In 1846, the exports were £149,484; the imports, £148,061.—The pop. in 1707 was 1,416 whites, and 2,861 Negroes. It was computed by Bryan Edwards in 1794, to amount to 4,000 whites and 21,000 Negroes. In 1836 it amounted to 1,612 whites, and 21,521 Negroes and people of colour. In 1838 it was reckoned at 22,482, of whom 4,952 were free males, and 5,739 apprenticed males.- There are 6 public schools. The colonists support 8 valuable livings, and above 12 Methodist and Moravian chapels. History.] This island was, at the period of its discovery by Columbus, well peopled by Charaibes, by whom it was called Liamuiga. It was the first of all the British settlements in the West Indies, having been settled in 1623, by a party of English, under Captain Thomas Warner; and soon afterwards a party of French arrived for the same purpose, under M. d'Esnambue. Warner's party lived at first on friendly terms with the natives, but having seized their lands, and being on this account apprehensive of an attack from them, they treacherously surprised the Charaibes by night, murdered from 100 to 120 of them, and expelled the rest from the island, reserving such of the women as were young and handsome. The colony, however, after this unjustifiable outrage against the natives, was far from flourishing; and the two leaders were deputed to their respective countries, for the purpose of bringing back recruits and supplies. Warner returned with about 400 settlers, and ample supplies; but d'Esnambue being ill provided with stores, lost the greater number of his party at sea, and the remainder arrived in a wretched condition. A treaty of partition was now signed, fixing the respective territories which each party should in future occupy: the upper part, called Capisterre, being allotted to the French; and the lower part, called Basseterre, to the English. The island was afterwards taken possession of by the Spaniards. In the course of wars between the two mother-countries, during the reign of Charles II., the English planters were attacked by the French, and expelled from their estates; in which, however, they were afterwards reinstated by the treaty of Breda. In 1689, when war broke out between France and Britain, the English settlers were again attacked and their estates laid waste by their French neighbours. After the latter had continued in possession of the island for eight months, it was retaken by a force under Colonel Codrington, and about 1,800 of the French were transported to Martinico and Hispaniola. Reparation was made to them by the treaty of Ryswick; but war breaking out between the two nations in 1702, the French planters only enjoyed a temporary possession of their estates. In 1705 the island was laid waste by a French armament, which committed such excesses that parliament was obliged to distribute £100,000 among the unhappy sufferers. This, however, was the last effect of civil discord and national hostility under which this little community was distracted; for, at the peace of Utrecht, the island was wholly ceded to the English, and the French possessions were publicly sold for the benefit of the English government. In 1772, a terrific hurricane destroyed £50,000 worth of property on this island. In 1782 St. C. was taken by a French armament under the command of the Marquis de Bouillé; but it was restored to Great Britain at the peace of 1783. In 1805, a large French force effected a landing near Basseterre, and levied a contribution of £18,000 upon the town.
CHRISTOPHSTHAL, a town of Wurtemburg, in the Schwarzwald circle, oberamt of Freudenstadt, on the Fohrbach. It contains several iron-works.
CHRISTOVAO SAO. See SERGIPE-DEL-REY.
CHRISTOW, a parish of Devon, <math>4\frac{1}{4}</math> m. NNW of Chudleigh, on the Teign. Area 3,680 acres. Pop. 624.
CHROPIN, a town of Moravia, in the circle and 8 m. SW of Prerau, on the l. bank of the March. Pop. 680. It contains a castle.

CHRUDIM, a circle and town of Austria, in Bohemia. The circle is 66 m. in length, and 27 m. in average breadth, forming a total superficies of 58.75 German or 1,247 English sq. m. It is divided into 9 townships, and 27 seignories; containing 14 towns, 21 market-towns, and 731 villages. Pop. in 1833, 303,916, of whom 21,676 were Protestants and Jews; and in 1837, 303,977. In the E and SE its surface is considerably elevated, and is covered with wood; but in other directions it consists of extensive plains, intersected with valleys, and watered by the Elbe and its tributaries, the Lauzna, and Chrudimka. In 1833, 258,993 jochs of this circle—which is reputed one of the richest and most productive in Bohemia-were under cultivation; 51,355 jochs were in meadow-land; 38,000 in pasturage; and 134,485 covered with forests. Its chief agricultural productions are wheat, rye, barley, oats, millet, legumes, potatoes, hemp, and lint. It is celebrated for its breed of horses. The rearing of cattle, sheep, pigs, and geese, forms also an important branch of rural industry. Iron and graphites are found in small quantities; but marbles of fine quality, limestone, and porcelain clay, are abundant. It possesses manufactories of linen, common cloth, wooden-ware, pottery, glass, &c., but excepting the first they are of little importance.—The town, which is the cap. of the circle, is situated on the r. bank of the Chrudimka, 21 m. S of Königgratz, and 66 m. ESE of Prague. Pop. in 1833, 5,712. It is surrounded by walls, and is well-built; and contains a magnificent church, a convent, and a normal school. It possesses some manufactories of common cloth, several tanneries, and a printing establishment, and has a fair for horses, which is reckoned the most important in Austria.

CHRUDIMKA, a river of Bohemia, which takes its rise in the SW of the circle of Chrudim, near the frontiers of Moravia; runs for 15 m. along the confines of the circle of Czaslau; waters Chrudim; and after a total course of 39 m., unites with the Elbe at Pardubitz. CHU
CHRYSTONE, a village in the p. of Cadder, in Lanarkshire. Pop. 555.
CHRZANOW, or Krzanow, a town of the republic and 28 m. WNW of Krakow, near the source of the Chechto. Pop. 1,230.
CHUAO, a maritime town of Venezuela, in the prov. and 50 m. W of Caracas, on the Caribbean sea.

CHUAPA, or HUANTILAUQUE, a river of Chile, which takes its rise in the Andes, near the volcano of the same name; runs W; receives the Illapel on the r.; and after a total course of 129 m., in which it forms the line of separation between the prov. of Coquimbo and Aconcagua, falls into the Pacific to the S of Point Ventama.
CHUASI, a summit of Northern Hindostan, in the prov. of Lahore, division of Sukhait, on the r. bank of the Sutledje, in N lat. 31° 25', E long. 78" 29'. Alt. 10,744 ft. above sea-level.
CHUBURNA, a town of Yucatan, on the gulf of Mexico, 23 m. N of Merida.
CHUBZEE-CHANG, a town of Tibet, in Tsang or Ulterior Tibet, 24 m. ENE of Chaprung.
CHUCABUCO, a town of Chile, in the prov. and 20 m. S of Aconcagua, and 55 m. E of Valparaiso.
CHUCH, a district and valley of the Punjab, to the E of Attock, about 20 m. in length from E to W, and 15 m. in breadth.
CHU-CHING-HYEN, or TCHOU-TCH'ING, a district and town of China, in the prov. of Shan-tung, division of Tsing-chu-fu. The town is in N lat. 36°, long. E of Peking 3° 29' 30".

CHU-CHU, a division and town of China, in the prov. of Gan-hwuy. The division comprises 2 districts, viz.: Tseen-tsuh-heën and Lae-gan-heën. CHU-CHU-FU, TCHU-TCHOU, or TCHHU-TCHEOO, a division and town of China, in the prov. of Chekeang. The division comprises 10 districts. The town lies on the N bank of the Ngew-kiang, 70 m. NW of the entrance of that river into the Tong-hai or Eastern sea, in N lat. 28° 25' 36".
CHUCHUNGA, a town of New Grenada, in the intendancy of Assuay, prov. and 50 m. NE of Jaen, on the l. bank of the Altummayo or Imassa, 8 m. SSE of its confluence with the Maranon.
CHUCKOWAL, or Chubkowal, a town of Northern India, on the Punjab, in N lat. 33° 5', 15 m. NW of Didwal.
CHUCKREALI, a village of the Punjab, 13 m. E of Jellalpur, and on the road from that town to Vizirabad.
CHUCUITO. See CHUQUITO.

CHUCUNAQUE, or CHUCUNQUE, a river of New Grenada, in the dep. of Ystmo, which rises near the source of the Ballano or Chepo, under the parallel of 8° 50' N; and flows S by E 60 m. to the Tuyra, which it joins on the r. bank, about 20 m. above its embouchure at the head of the gulf of San Miguel, on the Pacific coast. The C. in the upper part of its course approaches within 16 m. of the Atlantic side of the isthmus W of Cape Tiburon.
CHUDAVINAS, a tribe of aborigines in New Grenada, who inhabit the district to the S of the Pastaça, between 76° 30' and 77° 30′ W long.

CHUDLEIGH, a parish and market-town in Devonshire, 12 m. SSW of Exeter, on the E bank of the Teign. Area of p. 6,230 acres. Pop. 2,415. The town was nearly destroyed by fire in 1807. It chiefly consists of one street. It is a place of mere local trade, but famous for its cider. In the neighbourhood are extensive quarries of marble and limestone.

CHUDLEIGH, a village of Russia, in the gov. of Livonia, on the W shore of Lake Peipus, 24 m. S of Neinal. The lake is here bounded by steep cliffs of a yellowish limestone rock. On the r. bank of a rapid stream, which here falls into the lake, is the castle of C., "built by and named," says Erman, "after one of a body of emigrants from Scotland, some of whose descendants still possess estates round Yeva."
CHUDLEIGH (CAPE), the NW extremity of the peninsula of Labrador, in N lat. 60° 37', W long. 65°.
CHUELLES, a commune and village of France, in the dep. of Loiret, cant. of Chateau-Renard, 11 m. E of Montarges. Pop. 1,471.
CHUEN-PI, an island of China, in the Canton river, 23 m. above the island of Lintin, and 4 m. below Anunghoy.
CHUEN-SHA-PU, or Chuen-sha-ting, a town of China, in the prov. of Kiang-su, district of Songkiang-fu, 28 m. E by N of Song-kiang, on the coast of the Eastern sea.
CHUGHAI, a village of Abessinia, in the district of Matakkel, on the frontier of Aguamider, inhabited by Shankalas.

CHUGHEHIRAN, or JAUGHURI, a town of Afghanistan, in the district of Ghoraut, 135 m. N of Kandahar. CHUI, TCHUI, or TZUI, a river of Independent Tartary, rising in the Timourtou-nor, under the parallel of 42° 30', and flowing NW and W through the Kirghis steppes, passing through Lake Kolikol and Lake Kabankulak, after which it is supposed to prolong its course to the Karasu. It receives on the r. bank the Adji-Bakbulen; and on the l., the Almatu-Bulak, and the Little Chui; and has a probable course of 650 m. In spring its flood is so impetuous that it is not fordable; and this condition of its current lasts till the middle of July. In the course of the summer its bed is partially laid dry by the evaporation. Its banks are in many places covered with tall reeds, affording covert to tigers, ounces, and lynxes.

CHUKA, a fortress of Butan, in N lat. 27° 15', E long. 89° 35', 18 m. N of Murichom, on the l. bank of the Tchin-tchieu river. It is a large square stone building, with walls of great thickness. A little distance above the castle, the river is crossed by a bridge composed of five chains, supporting a platform covered with several layers of strong coarse bamboo mats. Captain Turner has given a plan and sections, and general view of this bridge, in his Embassy to Tibet.

CHUKALEO, a large lagune on the N coast of the gulf of Arta, in Albania, in N lat. 39° 4', E long. 20° 49', between the river Luro on the W, and Point Salahora on the E. CHU-KE, SHU-KE, or TCHU-KI, a district and town of China, in the prov. of Che-keang, division and 35 m. SW of Shaou-hing-fu, on an affluent of the Tcheng-tang-chiang, in N lat. 29° 45', long. E of Peking 3° 47′ 55".
CHUKERALA, a village of the Punjab, on the N side of the Jangher or Salt hills, and on the road from Kallabagh to Neilu, in N lat. 33° 3', E long. 72° 3′.
CHU-KI, a town of China, of the 3d class, in the prov. of Hu-pi, division of Yun-yang-fu, in N lat. 32° 10′, on an affluent of the Han-kiang.
CHUKLI, an Afshar village of Persia, in Azerdbijan, 12 m. S from Hisar, pleasantly situated on a small affluent of the Saruk.
CHUKURJI, a village of Turkey in Asia, 24 m. NW by W of Kutaiya.
CHULAR, a Turkoman village of Persia, in Kur- local trade. distan, 4 m. NW of Tatlar, near a pass in the Tash- Teller hills.
CHULLUNQUANI, a pass in the western cordillera of the Bolivian Andes, in S lat. 17° 18'. Alt. 15,610 ft .- Pentland.

CHU-LO, or KIA-Y, a district of China, in the prov. of Fo-kyen, island of Formoso, on the W coast, comprising a town of the same name, 4 Chinese and 33 native villages. The town is situated on an eminence | is in N lat. 22° 31', E long. 73° 41', 22 m. NE of CHU 36 m. N of Tae-wan, in N lat. 23° 27' 36", long. E of Peking 3° 44', on the S bank of the Nieou-tchaokhy. The estuary of this river forms a tolerably good harbour, named Lo-kang or Lookaun, with a bar over which is a depth of 2½ fath. Next to Tae-wan, C. is the most commercial place in the island. It is defended by fortifications and a ditch.
CHULUMANI, a province and town of Bolivia, in the dep. of La Paz, intersected by the Beni.—The town is 22 m. NNE of La Paz. Cocoa, betel, and coffee, are extensively cultivated in the environs.

CHULUWAN, an island on the E coast of Africa, 12 leagues S of Sofala. It is 16 m. in length, with a salt creek almost dividing it into two, and opening into the narrow channel that separates it from the main land.
CHUMALARI. See CHAMALARI.

CHUMBA, or Jumbo, a village of N India, in the Punjab, on the river Ravi, in N lat. 32° 22'. It is beautifully situated at the foot of a lofty mountain whose summit is covered with perpetual snow. Pop. 5,000.
CHUMBI, a village of Tibet, on the frontiers of the principality of Sikkim, 3 marches N of Tumlung.

CHUMBUL, or CHAMBAL, a river of Central India, which rises from a very elevated point of the Vendhya mountains, in three co-equal sources, the C., the Chumbela, and the Gumbhir. These three streams all descend from the N side of the Janapava; while no less than nine other streams, descending from the S side of the same cluster of hills, carry their tribute to the Nerbudda. The Sipra from Pipuldo, the Little Sinde from Dewas, and other minor streams passing Oujein [alt. 1,640 ft.] all unite with the C. as it flows N, and before it descends from the great central plateau of India. The Cali-Sinde from Baugri, the Newaz or Jamniri from Morsukri, and the Parbutti from the pass of Amlakhera, pursue rugged courses over the plateau, till engulphed in the C., on the r. bank, at the ferries of Nunera and Palli; where the river bends to the NW through the rugged district of Harrautti. On the l. bank, the C. receives the Bunass from the Aravulli, and the Beris from the lakes of Sodipur; and after watering Mewar, and skirting the S frontier of Jyepur, it bends S to join the Jumna, on the l. bank, 25 m. below Etawa. The course of the C., not reckoning its minor sinuosities, is upwards of 500 m.; and along its banks specimens of nearly every race now existing in India may be found. [Tod.] It is navigable only a short distance above its junction with the Jumna.—Major Rennell supposes this river to be the Sambus of Arrian.

CHUMIE MOUNTAINS, a ridge in S Africa, among the head-streams of the Kat river, intersected by the parallel of 32° 35', and the meridian of 26° 48' E.—A river of the same name descends from the Katherg and the SE flanks of this range, and flows SSE to the Keiskamma; and a missionary settlement has been formed on an affluent of the C., 15 m. ENE of Fort Beaufort.
CHUMLEIGH, a parish and market-town in Devonshire, 21 m. NW of Exeter. Area of p. 8,650 acres. Pop. 1,647.—The town is situated on an eminence near the river Taw. It has only a small

CHUMOREREEL LAKE, a beautiful sheet of water in Chinese Tartary, 8 or 10 m. long, and 4 or 5 m. broad, whence the Para, one of the head-streams of the Spiti, flows S to its junction with the Zungcham, in N lat. 33° 4'.

CHUMPANIR, or CHANPANIR, a district of Hindostan, forming the E frontier of Gujerat, lying between the Mahe and the Nerbudda rivers. Its cap. Baroda. Its citadel, on the summit of a mountain rising 2,500 ft. above the level of the surrounding country, is visible from Ahmedabad 70 m. distant. The town does not contain above 300 houses, but ruins of ancient buildings extend for several miles on each side of the mountain. The territory of C. is principally held by the British government, and the Guicowar. A few petty chiefs still maintain a kind of feudal dependency on the border of the sacred citadel of C., the seat of the goddess Cali.
CHUMPAWUT, or CHAMPAVATI, a town in N Hindostan, the ancient cap. of Kumaon, in N lat. 29° 20', 107 m. N by E of Bareilly. It is a small decayed place.

CHUNAR, or CHANDALGURH, a town and fortress of Hindostan, in the district of Mirzapore, and prov. of Allahabad, on the S bank of the Ganges, 13 m. SW of Benares, in N lat. 25° 9', 574 m. travelling distance from Calcutta. The fortress occupies the summit and sides of a rock surrounded on all sides by steep precipices. The face towards the river is particularly formidable, and projects boldly into the water. The whole is flanked, wherever it has been possible to obtain a salient angle, with towers, bartizans, and bastions, of various forms and sizes. On the last enclosure of the fortress, on the very summit of the mountain, are the remains of a very ancient Hindu palace; and a small square court, overshadowed by a pepul tree, under which is a large slab of marble on which the Hindus believe "the Almighty is seated personally though invisibly for 9 hours every day, removing during the other 3 hours to Benares." [Heber.] The houses belonging to the European residents are prettily situated on a steep declivity, to the E of the fort, luxuriantly clothed with trees, and covered with orchards and gardens; the native town crowns the summit beyond. The houses of the native town are mostly of two stories, and generally with verandahs in front which are let out into shops: the whole, says Bishop Heber, "not unlike a Welsh market-town, but much larger, and probably containing 15,000 people." Many of the buildings are of stone, of which there are fine quarries in the neighbourhood. C. vies with Benares in the sanctity of its character in the estimation of Hindus. The surrounding country is of a highly romantic character; but the place has lost its importance as a military station, and now forms an asylum for such invalid soldiers as are equal to the performance of garrison-duty.
CHUNAT, a township in the p. of Glossop, in Derbyshire, 4 m. from Hayfield. Pop. 111.

CHUNCHOS, a formidable tribe of Indians who inhabit the S part of the Pampa-del-Sacramento in Peru, and chiefly the district watered by the Chanchamayo and the Perene. They have their chief residence in Chibatizo, 9 leagues from Pucara. They are in possession of a rich bed of salt, some 12 or 14 leagues from Vitoc. They dye their hair a deep vermilion, and paint their face and breast the same colour. Their weapons consist of a bow and poisoned arrows and a large wooden sword.
CHUNDAIL. See CHANDEL.
CHUNDERBUDUNI, a mountain in N Hindostan, 11 m. NW of Serinagur, in N lat. 30° 19'. Alt. 7,661 ft. above sea-level.
CHUNDOWSY. See CHANDAUSI.
CHUNG-SIANG, or Tchoung-Thsiang, a town of China, of the 3d class, in the prov. of Hu-pi, division of Ngan-lo-fu.
CHUNG-WEI, or TCHOUNG-WEI, a district and town of China, in the prov. of Kan-su, division of Ning-hia-fu, between the Hoang-ho river and the Great wall, 80 m. SW of Ninghia.

CHUN-KING-FU, or Tchong-King, a division and town of China, in the prov. of Sze-chuen. The division comprises 24 districts. The town is situated CHU Yang-tse-kiang, 90 m. SSE of Shun-king-fu, in N lat. 29° 42′ 10". It rises amphitheatrally on the declivity of a mountain, and is well although irregularly built. It is noted for its sugar-refineries and its manufactories of cane boxes, and possesses a very active trade. In the vicinity is a convent, celebrated for its wealth, and containing, it is reported, an extensive collection of books.
CHUN-TEEN-TING, or TCHOUNG-TIEN, a district and town of China, in the prov. of Yun-nan, division and NW of Le-keang.

CHUNTOP, a river of SW Africa, in the Damara territory, which rises under the parallel of 24° S, and flows NNW through a hilly region abounding in game, for about 30 m., when it turns W, and appears to be lost in sands before it reaches the coast.

CHUPARA, or Chuparas Point, a promontory of the island of Trinidad, on the NW coast, in N lat. 10° 49′ 15", and W long. 61° 25', forming the E side of the channel named Dragon's Mouth, leading into the gulf of Paria.

CHUPAT, a river of Patagonia, on the E coast, flowing into the Atlantic in S lat. 43° 20' 45", W long. 65° 02′ 50" [Fitzroy.] Its entrance will not admit a vessel drawing more than 7 ft., and even this only at high water. Within the bar it is shoal and narrow, in few places exceeding 100 yards in breadth, and 6 ft. in depth at low water. The banks present rich tracts of low alluvial land, and huge quantities of drift timber. As traced from an elevated point about 12 m. from its mouth, it appears to flow in a very serpentine course through level meadow land covered with rich herbage, on which large herds of wild cattle graze. Captain Fitzroy says that, judging from the drift-timber, as well as volcanic scoriæ brought down by this river, there is ground for supposing that it may rise in the Cordillera.
CHUPPRA, a town and pergunnah of Hindostan, in the prov. of Malwa, 110 m. NE of Ougein, and 70 m. SE of Kota.

CHUPRA, a town of Hindostan, cap. of the district of Sarun, in the prov. of Bahar, on the N bank of the Ganges, or rather on an arm of that river divided from the main stream by some marshy islands, 35 m. WNW of Patna, in N lat. 25° 46', and E long. 84° 44'. Pop. about 30,000. It extends in a narrow line along the river for nearly 1 m., contains a good many large houses, and possesses an active trade in sugar and cotton. The Kharwa tribe frequent the environs in considerable numbers.

CHUQUIBAMBA, a colossal nevado or snowy mountain of the western cordillera of the Bolivian Andes, so called from a large village situated at its base 21 m. from Arequipa. The nevado has, under certain points of view, a striking resemblance to Chimborazo. Its dome rises to an alt. of 21,000 ft., as deduced from a measurement Mr. Pentland made of that portion of its summit which rises above the limit of perpetual snow, assuming this line at 17,200 ft., in the parallel of 16°. Like Chimborazo also, it appears to be formed of one simultaneously uplifted mass of trachyte, which has pierced and reposes upon the subjacent secondary strata of new red sandstone, with saliferous and gypseous marls.

CHUQUISACA, or CHARCAS, a department of Bolivia, lying between the parallels of 19° and 22° 30' S, and comprising the mountainous territory stretching from the upper part of the course of the Rio-Grande-de-la-Plata on the N, to the Pilcomayo on the S. On the W it is bounded by the llanos of Chaco; on the E it is conterminous with the dep. of Potosi. The Parapiti, the Cachamayo, and the Paspaya rivers intersect it. Its valleys are wide and fertile, especially those of the W section; but lose at the confluence of the Kia-ling-kiang, with the | their fertility as they sink down towards the level of the llanos. Its area is 23,870 sq. m.; and its pop. was recently estimated at 156,000.—The capital, of the same name, and the seat of the general government of Bolivia, is situated in S lat. 19° 29', on the 1. bank of the Cachimayo, 50 m. ENE of Potosi, at an alt. of about 9,000 ft. above sea-level. Its pop. is variously estimated at from 15,000 to 25,000. Miller estimated it in 1826 at 18,000. It has a large and handsome cathedral, and several educational institutions, among which are a university, and a mining school.—C. was founded in 1539; and in 1559 was made the seat of the audiencia of Los Charcas. In 1608 it became a metropolitan see, the jurisdiction of its archbishop extending over the whole viceroyalty of Buenos Ayres, with the exception of the prov. of Cayo. On the 6th of August 1825, the Bolivian congress assembled here issued their declaration of independence. It has successively borne the names of La Plata, and Charcas.

CHUQUITO, a town of Peru, in the dep. of Puno, near the W shores of Lake Titicaca. Pop. 5,000. It has a magnificent church and handsome marketplace. See TITICACA. CHUR, Coire, or Coira, a town of Switzerland, capital of the cant. of Grisons, 63 m. SE of Zurich, 60 m. ESE of Luzern, and 120 m. E of Berne. It is situated in a fertile valley of the Ligne-Caddée, and of the jurisdiction of the same name, on the r. bank of the Plessur, 3 m. above its confluence with the Rhine, at an alt. of 1,970 ft. above sea-level, in N lat. 46° 50', E long. 9° 26'. Pop. 5,489, chiefly Protestant. It is surrounded by an old wall and ditch; and consists of two parts, the upper of which contains the cathedral, an edifice of the 8th cent., and the episcopal palace. The only other buildings worthy of note are St. Martin's church, the town-house, in which is the public library, the museum, the bank, and the episcopal seminary. There are besides two cantonal schools, a medical school, an alms-house, and several other benevolent institutions. The streets, narrow and tortuous, present a generally sombre aspect. It possesses an active entrepot trade with the eastern part of Germany and with Italy. The environs are highly picturesque, and contain some mineral springs.—C., the Curia Rhætorum of the Romans, was founded about the middle of the 4th cent., and before 452 had been erected into an episcopal see. It was the birthplace of the paintress Angelica Kauffmann.

CHUR, a mountain-group in the Himalaya system, in N lat. 30° 52′, which divides the hill provinces of Sirmur and Jubul. Its alt. was determined by Hodgson and Herbert to be 11,689 ft. above sealevel. It is seen at a great distance from every point; and the streams which descend from it flow to the Girri on the one side, and the Pabur on the other. The snow lies deep on its N side till the commencement of the rains in June, when it becomes shrouded in mist and clouds. The summit is composed of vast tubular masses of compact granite, much decomposed in many places. Ridges radiate from the central nucleus in all directions, composed of successive strata of mica-slate, followed by clayslate containing a rich iron ore. Galena also extensively occurs near the junction of the clay and micaslate. The botany of the C. is in many respects the same with the main range of snowy cliffs to which it is united by a continuous ridge of nearly 8,000 ft. in elevation at the source of the Girri. On the summit appear the juniper, the Alpine rhododendron, and the aconite. The sides are clothed with a dark and dense pine forest, many of the trees of which are immense both in girth and height. Vigne was told that the minarets of Delhi are sometimes visible on the horizon from the summit of the C. II. CHU
CHURCH (STATES OF THE). See PAPAL STATES.
CHURCH, a township in the p. of Llanbister, Radnorshire. Pop. 378.—Also a township in the p. of Llandewy-Ystradenny. Pop. 336.—Also a tything in the p. of Downton, Wilts. Pop. 319.
CHURCHAM, a parish of Gloucestershire, 41 m. SW of Gloucester, N of the Severn. Area 4,100 acres. Pop. 870.
CHURCH-ASTON, a chapelry in the p. of Edgmond, Salop, about a mile S of Newport. Pop. 512.
CHURCH-BERE. See BERE-CHURCH.
CHURCH - BRAMPTON. See BRAMPTON - CHURCH.
CHURCH - BROUGHTON. See BROUGHTONCHURCH.
CHURCH-CONISTONE, a chapelry in the p. of and 12 m. from Ulverstone, Lancashire, and 4 m. from Hawkshead. Area 7,210 acres. Pop. 1,148.
CHURCH-COPPENHALL. See COPPENHALL.
CHURCH-CREEK, a village in Dorchester co., in the state of Maryland, U.S., 69 m. SE of Annapolis.
CHURCH-DILWYN, a township in the p. of Dilwyn, Herefordshire. Pop. 373.
CHURCHDOWN, a parish of Gloucestershire, 3\frac{1}{2} m. E of Gloucester, on the Cheltenham and Gloucester railway. Area 4,070 acres. Pop. 999.
CHURCH-EATON, a parish of Staffordshire, 7 m. E of Newport, and near the Birmingham and Liverpool canal. Area 4,930 acres. Pop. 743.
